***The Amazing Spider-Man*** is a 2012 American [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") based on the [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") character [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man "Spider-Man"). Directed by [Marc Webb](/wiki/Marc_Webb "Marc Webb") and written by [James Vanderbilt](/wiki/James_Vanderbilt "James Vanderbilt"), [Alvin Sargent](/wiki/Alvin_Sargent "Alvin Sargent"), and [Steve Kloves](/wiki/Steve_Kloves "Steve Kloves") from a story by Vanderbilt, it is a [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)") of the [*Spider-Man* film series](/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film "Spider-Man in film"). It stars [Andrew Garfield](/wiki/Andrew_Garfield "Andrew Garfield"), [Emma Stone](/wiki/Emma_Stone "Emma Stone"), [Rhys Ifans](/wiki/Rhys_Ifans "Rhys Ifans"), [Denis Leary](/wiki/Denis_Leary "Denis Leary"), [Campbell Scott](/wiki/Campbell_Scott "Campbell Scott"), [Irrfan Khan](/wiki/Irrfan_Khan "Irrfan Khan"), [Martin Sheen](/wiki/Martin_Sheen "Martin Sheen"), and [Sally Field](/wiki/Sally_Field "Sally Field"). The plot of the film follows the teenager [Peter Parker](/wiki/Peter_Parker_(The_Amazing_Spider-Man_film_series) "Peter Parker (The Amazing Spider-Man film series)") (Garfield) who gains spider-like abilities and fights crime as Spider-Man, while also attempting to live an ordinary life.

Development of the film began in 2010 following the cancellation of *Spider-Man 4*, which would have been the fourth film in [Sam Raimi](/wiki/Sam_Raimi "Sam Raimi")'s *Spider-Man* series starring [Tobey Maguire](/wiki/Tobey_Maguire "Tobey Maguire"). [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") opted to reboot the franchise with the same production team, with Vanderbilt staying on to write, and Sargent and Kloves assisting with the script. Using the [RED Epic camera](/wiki/Red_Digital_Cinema_Camera_Company#Cameras "Red Digital Cinema Camera Company"), [principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") occurred in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and New York City between December 2010 and May 2011. [3ality Technica](/wiki/3ality_Technica "3ality Technica") provided 3D image processing, while [Sony Pictures Imageworks](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Imageworks "Sony Pictures Imageworks") handled [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") effects. It was the last film written by Sargent before his death in 2019.

[Sony Pictures Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Entertainment") promoted the film with a [viral marketing](/wiki/Viral_marketing "Viral marketing") campaign and tie-ins including a [video game](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(2012_video_game) "The Amazing Spider-Man (2012 video game)") by [Beenox](/wiki/Beenox "Beenox") and [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision"). *The Amazing Spider-Man* premiered in Tokyo on June 13, 2012, and was released in 2D, [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film"), [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") 3D, and [4DX](/wiki/4DX "4DX") formats in the United States on July 3 by [Sony Pictures Releasing](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Releasing "Sony Pictures Releasing") through its Columbia Pictures label. The film grossed $758.7 million worldwide, making it the [seventh-highest-grossing film of 2012](/wiki/2012_in_film#Highest-grossing_films "2012 in film"). A sequel, *[The Amazing Spider-Man 2](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_2 "The Amazing Spider-Man 2")*, was released in 2014. In 2021, Garfield and Ifans reprised their roles in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") film *[Spider-Man: No Way Home](/wiki/Spider-Man:_No_Way_Home "Spider-Man: No Way Home")*, which explored the concept of the [multiverse](/wiki/Multiverse_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Multiverse (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") and linked the *Spider-Man* series directed by [Jon Watts](/wiki/Jon_Watts "Jon Watts") to the films directed by Webb and Raimi.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

After discovering that his home office has been burglarized, scientist [Richard Parker](/wiki/Richard_and_Mary_Parker "Richard and Mary Parker") and his wife Mary flee to an unknown destination, leaving their young son [Peter](/wiki/Peter_Parker_(The_Amazing_Spider-Man_film_series) "Peter Parker (The Amazing Spider-Man film series)") in the care of his [Aunt May](/wiki/Aunt_May "Aunt May") and [Uncle Ben](/wiki/Uncle_Ben "Uncle Ben").

Years later, a teenage Peter attends [Midtown Science High School](/wiki/Midtown_High_School_(comics) "Midtown High School (comics)") in New York City. He is intelligent, but socially awkward and often bullied. He has a crush on [Gwen Stacy](/wiki/Gwen_Stacy_(The_Amazing_Spider-Man_film_series) "Gwen Stacy (The Amazing Spider-Man film series)"), who returns his feelings. Peter discovers his father's briefcase and learns from Ben that his father was a friend of [Dr. Curt Connors](/wiki/Curt_Connors_(The_Amazing_Spider-Man_film_series) "Curt Connors (The Amazing Spider-Man film series)"), a scientist at [Oscorp](/wiki/Oscorp "Oscorp") in the field of [cross-species genetics](/wiki/Genetic_engineering "Genetic engineering"). Hoping to learn about his father's work, Peter sneaks into a restricted laboratory at Oscorp, where a genetically modified spider bites him. He develops spider-like abilities, including superhuman strength, agility, speed, and the ability to cling to surfaces.

Connors, whose right arm has been amputated, is conducting experiments to regenerate limbs. Peter discovers the missing piece to Connors' research, the "decay rate algorithm", in his father's papers. Peter visits Connors, reveals that he is Richard's son, and gives Connors the algorithm. At home, Peter and Ben argue, and Peter leaves. While searching for Peter, Ben is killed by a thief. Under the alias Spider-Man, Peter uses his new abilities to track down criminals matching the killer's description. He creates a mask with yellow-tinted glasses and a [spandex](/wiki/Spandex "Spandex") suit to hide his identity, and builds mechanical web-shooters using a web formula developed by Oscorp. At dinner with Gwen's family, he discovers her father is police captain [George Stacy](/wiki/George_Stacy "George Stacy"), who holds a grudge against Spider-Man. After arguing with Captain Stacy, Peter reveals his double identity to Gwen, and they kiss.

Connors' superior Rajit Ratha demands that Connors begin human trials with his formula to regrow limbs using lizard DNA. Connors refuses to rush into testing, as it could put innocent people at risk. Ratha fires Connors and decides to test the serum at a [Veterans Administration](/wiki/Veterans_Administration "Veterans Administration") hospital. Desperate, Connors tries the formula on himself, passes out, and awakens to find that his missing arm has regenerated. While trying to intercept Ratha on the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), Connors turns into a humanoid reptilian monster and begins a violent rampage; Peter arrives and saves people from Connors' attacks. Unable to piece together a narrative for the incident, the police issue a city-wide manhunt for both the Lizard and Spider-Man.

Connors discovers that Peter is Spider-Man, and attacks him at school. After Peter chases him off, he pursues Connors into the sewers, where he finds a makeshift lab. He realizes that Connors is planning to release a biochemical agent from Oscorp Tower, which will turn everyone in the city into reptilian hybrids like himself. As Peter tries to stop Connors, the police corner him, and Captain Stacy discovers his double identity. Stacy lets Peter go after Connors, while Gwen creates an antidote for Connors' chemical agent. While Stacy occupies Connors in battle, Peter disperses the antidote into the city, curing Connors and his victims. Dying from wounds inflicted by Connors, Stacy asks Peter to stay away from Gwen to keep her safe. Peter tries to honor this request, but realizes both he and Gwen are unhappy, and he hints to her that he may not keep his promise.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

Further information: [Spider-Man 4](/wiki/Spider-Man_4 "Spider-Man 4")

Following the release of [Sam Raimi](/wiki/Sam_Raimi "Sam Raimi")'s *[Spider-Man 3](/wiki/Spider-Man_3 "Spider-Man 3")* (2007), [Sony Pictures Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Entertainment") announced a May 5, 2011 release date for the next film in [the series](/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film#Sam_Raimi_trilogy "Spider-Man in film"). By this time, [James Vanderbilt](/wiki/James_Vanderbilt "James Vanderbilt"), [David Lindsay-Abaire](/wiki/David_Lindsay-Abaire "David Lindsay-Abaire") and [Gary Ross](/wiki/Gary_Ross "Gary Ross") had written several screenplays for the film, which were rejected by Raimi. [Alvin Sargent](/wiki/Alvin_Sargent "Alvin Sargent"), who co-wrote *Spider-Man 2* and *Spider-Man 3*, was working on yet another script.[[27]](#cite_note-deadline01-05-10-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33) On January 11, 2010, [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") and [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") announced that instead of continuing the Raimi series, they were rebooting the series with a new cast and crew. *Deadline Hollywood* reported that Raimi had pulled out of the project, and the studio did not want to continue the series without him. Additionally, [Avi Arad](/wiki/Avi_Arad "Avi Arad") felt that the writers had not produced a story of sufficient quality to warrant a continuation of the Raimi series.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-34) Arad, Matt Tolmach and [Laura Ziskin](/wiki/Laura_Ziskin "Laura Ziskin") were retained as producers for the upcoming reboot.[[30]](#cite_note-deadline-35) According to Tolmach, the producers felt the core story of Spider-Man was that of a boy becoming a man, which could be depicted again in the reboot.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-34)

Tolmach, now president of Columbia Pictures, and [Amy Pascal](/wiki/Amy_Pascal "Amy Pascal"), co-chairman of Sony Pictures Entertainment, sought a director who could give sharp focus to Peter Parker's life.[[31]](#cite_note-Webb-36)[[32]](#cite_note-Webb_2-37) [Kathryn Bigelow](/wiki/Kathryn_Bigelow "Kathryn Bigelow") and [David Fincher](/wiki/David_Fincher "David Fincher") were considered for the job before Marc Webb was selected. Webb had recently made his directorial debut with *[500 Days of Summer](/wiki/500_Days_of_Summer "500 Days of Summer")* (2009).[[f]](#cite_note-40) Webb praised Raimi's version of the Spider-Man character and said it was a challenging precedent to follow. He did not want to tell another story with Raimi's version of the character, but instead wanted to present a new version of Spider-Man.[[g]](#cite_note-43) He later said that the world of *The Amazing Spider-Man* was influenced by the *[Ultimate Spider-Man](/wiki/Ultimate_Spider-Man "Ultimate Spider-Man")* comic series, but was also dreamed up by the filmmakers.[[37]](#cite_note-EW-Brunner-44) The finalized screenplay was written by Vanderbilt and Sargent, with contributions from [Steve Kloves](/wiki/Steve_Kloves "Steve Kloves") and [Paul Feig](/wiki/Paul_Feig "Paul Feig").[[38]](#cite_note-Klove-45)[[39]](#cite_note-46)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Casting")]

#### Peter Parker / Spider-Man

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Peter Parker / Spider-Man")]

Before Andrew Garfield was cast as Spider-Man, other actors were considered for the role, including [Michael Angarano](/wiki/Michael_Angarano "Michael Angarano"), [Drake Bell](/wiki/Drake_Bell "Drake Bell"), [Jamie Bell](/wiki/Jamie_Bell "Jamie Bell"), [Frank Dillane](/wiki/Frank_Dillane "Frank Dillane"), [Alden Ehrenreich](/wiki/Alden_Ehrenreich "Alden Ehrenreich"), [Josh Hutcherson](/wiki/Josh_Hutcherson "Josh Hutcherson"), [Aaron Johnson](/wiki/Aaron_Taylor-Johnson "Aaron Taylor-Johnson"), [Joe Jonas](/wiki/Joe_Jonas "Joe Jonas"), [Logan Lerman](/wiki/Logan_Lerman "Logan Lerman") and [Anton Yelchin](/wiki/Anton_Yelchin "Anton Yelchin").[[h]](#cite_note-51) On July 1, 2010, it was announced that the role would go to Garfield, who said he calmed his anxiety about getting the part by pretending he was auditioning for a *Spider-Man* short film made by his friends.[[44]](#cite_note-comingsoon-52)[[45]](#cite_note-53) The film's director, Marc Webb, said that Garfield brought humor, emotional weight and physicality to the role. Garfield and Emma Stone were screen-tested together, and Webb said there was instant chemistry between them; they began dating while shooting the film.[[46]](#cite_note-54)[[47]](#cite_note-chemistry_EW-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Andrew_Garfield_by_Gage_Skidmore_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Andrew_Garfield_by_Gage_Skidmore_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrew_Garfield_by_Gage_Skidmore_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Emma_Stone_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Emma_Stone_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emma_Stone_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Andrew Garfield (left) and Emma Stone at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") in 2013

Garfield described Peter as someone he can relate to, and said the character had been an important influence on him since he was a young boy.[[i]](#cite_note-58) To prepare for the role, he studied the movements of both athletes and spiders, and incorporated spider-like movements into his performance of Peter, even when the character is not wearing his Spider-Man suit.[[50]](#cite_note-Spider-59)[[51]](#cite_note-NPR-60) Garfield practiced [yoga](/wiki/Yoga "Yoga") and [Pilates](/wiki/Pilates "Pilates") in order to be as flexible as possible. He said the physical training for the role was demanding and exhausting; he had three different stunt doubles during filming.[[52]](#cite_note-61)[[53]](#cite_note-62) Garfield admitted to shedding a tear when he first wore the Spider-Man costume, and that he tried to imagine a "better" actor in the suit, because seeing himself as Spider-Man did not make sense to him.[[54]](#cite_note-suit-63) He said the suit was uncomfortable and that he was not allowed to wear anything underneath except underwear, because it was skintight.[[55]](#cite_note-64)

Webb described *The Amazing Spider-Man* as "a story about a kid who grows up looking for his father and finds himself." Both Webb and Garfield described Peter as an outsider, with Webb noting that in the early *Spider-Man* comics, his outsider status was defined by him being a nerdy "science whiz".[[56]](#cite_note-65)[[57]](#cite_note-66) Webb said that because the idea of "what a nerd is" has changed, and because "nerds" are now "running the world", a challenge of the film was how to define Peter's outsider status in a contemporary context.[[58]](#cite_note-latimes-67)[[59]](#cite_note-68) Garfield tried to capture how Peter "copes with the feelings of being an underdog, his confusions and his self-doubt" in an attempt to bring "a certain empathy and sensitivity to his character that explains how it helps him become the hero".[[60]](#cite_note-orphan2-69) He said that Peter goes through normal struggles that most people experience, and described him as "a very inspiring, aspirational character that symbolizes goodness—and how difficult it is to be good".[[51]](#cite_note-NPR-60) Garfield called Peter's Spider-Man costume a "protective layer" which gives him a powerful anonymity, allowing him to become witty and "get away with anything." Garfield compared the costume to the anonymity of the Internet.[[61]](#cite_note-troll-70)[[62]](#cite_note-orphan-71)

#### Gwen Stacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Gwen Stacy")]

In September 2010, it was reported that Sony was casting two female lead roles, leading to speculation that both [Mary Jane Watson](/wiki/Mary_Jane_Watson "Mary Jane Watson") and Gwen Stacy would appear in the film. However, *[The Wrap](/wiki/TheWrap "TheWrap")* reported in November that Mary Jane was never in the script.[[63]](#cite_note-Sperling-72)[[64]](#cite_note-73) Candidates for the role of Gwen included [Dianna Agron](/wiki/Dianna_Agron "Dianna Agron"), [Lily Collins](/wiki/Lily_Collins "Lily Collins"), [Georgina Haig](/wiki/Georgina_Haig "Georgina Haig"), [Ophelia Lovibond](/wiki/Ophelia_Lovibond "Ophelia Lovibond"), [Dominique McElligott](/wiki/Dominique_McElligott "Dominique McElligott"), [Imogen Poots](/wiki/Imogen_Poots "Imogen Poots") and [Mia Wasikowska](/wiki/Mia_Wasikowska "Mia Wasikowska").[[j]](#cite_note-77) By early October, Emma Stone had been chosen for the role, due in large part to her chemistry with Garfield.[[8]](#cite_note-Emma_Stone-9) Stone was unfamiliar with the *Spider-Man* comics, and educated herself in preparation for the role. She described Gwen as "a daddy's girl" who is very responsible and protective of her family. She said Gwen offers Peter stability and "a family unit not marred with parental loss".[[k]](#cite_note-81) Stone said she wanted to interpret the character in her own way, but hoped her performance would please fans of the comics.[[l]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Rhys_Ifans_2011_cropped_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Rhys_Ifans_2011_cropped_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhys_Ifans_2011_cropped_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Denis_Leary_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Denis_Leary_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Denis_Leary_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Rhys Ifans (left, pictured in 2011), and Denis Leary (in 2015)

#### Curt Connors / Lizard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Curt Connors / Lizard")]

By mid-October 2010, [Rhys Ifans](/wiki/Rhys_Ifans "Rhys Ifans") was confirmed for the role of Curt Connors / Lizard; [Michael Fassbender](/wiki/Michael_Fassbender "Michael Fassbender") was also considered for the role.[[m]](#cite_note-90) Webb has stated that Connors is not a traditional villain, while Ifans has claimed he does not qualify as a villain at all. Ifans described him as a flawed and "broken" man who attempts to help others and himself but makes a bad decision.[[n]](#cite_note-95) He compared Connors to [Dr. Jekyll](/wiki/Strange_Case_of_Dr_Jekyll_and_Mr_Hyde "Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde"), and viewed his transformation into the Lizard as an experience of great euphoria and hubris, similar to that of a drug addict.[[82]](#cite_note-:3-96)[[83]](#cite_note-Mr._Hyde-97) When preparing for the role, Ifans purposely did not read many of the *Spider-Man* comics. When designing the Lizard, Webb chose not to give him a lizard snout, as he wanted him to appear human as well as monstrous, and be able to convey emotions.[[84]](#cite_note-Webb_trailer_2-98)[[85]](#cite_note-design-99)

#### Other characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other characters")]

In November, [Denis Leary](/wiki/Denis_Leary "Denis Leary") accepted the role of George Stacy, and Martin Sheen was confirmed for the role of Uncle Ben.[[o]](#cite_note-103) Sheen described Ben as a surrogate father for Peter, and Webb praised Sheen for bringing a "benevolent authority" to the role.[[89]](#cite_note-Sheen_3-104) Webb sought to portray Ben as a [blue-collar](/wiki/Blue-collar_worker "Blue-collar worker") man, whose outlook on life was different from the science-inclined Peter. Webb found it interesting to explore the differences between Peter and his aunt and uncle.[[84]](#cite_note-Webb_trailer_2-98)

Sally Field said she took the role of Aunt May as a favor to her friend [Laura Ziskin](/wiki/Laura_Ziskin "Laura Ziskin"), because she knew it would be Ziskin's last film (Ziskin died in 2011).[[90]](#cite_note-105)[[91]](#cite_note-106) Webb praised Field for bringing "genuine affection" to the role.[[84]](#cite_note-Webb_trailer_2-98) Irrfan Khan said he was offered the role of Rajit Ratha[[p]](#cite_note-108) after appearing in the television drama *[In Treatment](/wiki/In_Treatment_(American_TV_series) "In Treatment (American TV series)")*.[[93]](#cite_note-Khan_2-109) Webb was a fan of Khan after watching the series, along with Khan's films *[The Namesake](/wiki/The_Namesake_(film) "The Namesake (film)")* and *[The Warrior](/wiki/The_Warrior_(2001_British_film) "The Warrior (2001 British film)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-Oscorp-16) Khan said his sons were excited about the role and insisted he take it.[[94]](#cite_note-Khan_3-110)

### Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Design")]

Webb felt a responsibility to reinvent Spider-Man.[[58]](#cite_note-latimes-67) One departure from the preceding trilogy was to have Spider-Man build artificial web-shooters, as he does in the comics. Webb explained that the web-shooters in *The Amazing Spider-Man* were meant to showcase Peter's intelligence. The crew also redesigned Spider-Man's costume for the film, in an attempt to make Peter's body appear "longer and more lithe, more of an acrobat, someone incredibly agile". Webb wanted it to be apparent that the costume and web-shooters were things that Peter constructed himself. The crew created multiple different Spider-Man suits for various lighting conditions.[[95]](#cite_note-web_shooters-111)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Filming")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Above_Williamsburg_Bridge_crop.jpg/250px-Above_Williamsburg_Bridge_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Above_Williamsburg_Bridge_crop.jpg)

The scene in which the Lizard rampages on the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") was filmed both on the bridge itself and on a Los Angeles soundstage, where a 150-foot piece of the bridge was constructed.[[96]](#cite_note-filmjournal-112)

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") for *The Amazing Spider-Man* occurred in Los Angeles and New York City. Filming began on December 6, 2010. Locations in Los Angeles included the [Henry Fonda Theater](/wiki/Music_Box_Theater_(Los_Angeles) "Music Box Theater (Los Angeles)") in Hollywood, the gym of [St. John Bosco High School](/wiki/St._John_Bosco_High_School "St. John Bosco High School"), [Immanuel Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Immanuel_Presbyterian_Church_(Los_Angeles,_California) "Immanuel Presbyterian Church (Los Angeles, California)") in [Mid-Wilshire](/wiki/Mid-Wilshire "Mid-Wilshire"), and various locations around [South Pasadena](/wiki/South_Pasadena,_California "South Pasadena, California"), [San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles "San Pedro, Los Angeles") and [Woodland Hills](/wiki/Woodland_Hills,_Los_Angeles "Woodland Hills, Los Angeles").[[97]](#cite_note-RED2-113)[[98]](#cite_note-LA-114) In New York, the [Alexander Hamilton U.S. Custom House](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_U.S._Custom_House "Alexander Hamilton U.S. Custom House") served as the exterior for [NYPD](/wiki/NYPD "NYPD") headquarters, while an apartment on Manhattan's [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side") was used as the exterior of the Stacy family home. A row of houses on Fuller Place in the Brooklyn neighborhood of [Windsor Terrace](/wiki/Windsor_Terrace,_Brooklyn "Windsor Terrace, Brooklyn") stood in for the [Forest Hills, Queens](/wiki/Forest_Hills,_Queens "Forest Hills, Queens") neighborhood of Ben and May Parker. A web-swinging stunt sequence was filmed along the [Riverside Drive Viaduct](/wiki/Riverside_Drive_Viaduct "Riverside Drive Viaduct") in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem").[[99]](#cite_note-115)[[100]](#cite_note-116) Principal photography wrapped in May 2011, with reshoots taking place in New York that November and in Los Angeles in December.[[q]](#cite_note-120)

For action sequences involving Spider-Man, Webb relied more on stunt performers than previous *Spider-Man* films, which had used extensive CGI to create the character's superhuman movements.[[104]](#cite_note-Webb_rant-121)[[105]](#cite_note-RT-122) To determine how Spider-Man should look when swinging from his webs, stunt coordinator Andy Armstrong studied the movements of an [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") gymnast.[[r]](#cite_note-126) Garfield performed many of his own stunts, which required him to undertake rigorous physical training and lessons in [parkour](/wiki/Parkour "Parkour").[[109]](#cite_note-127) The crew spent months creating rigs that would allow Garfield and his stunt doubles to swing in a way that was not computer-generated.[[58]](#cite_note-latimes-67) One rig on [Riverside Drive](/wiki/Riverside_Drive_(Manhattan) "Riverside Drive (Manhattan)") in Harlem was hundreds of feet long, and allowed a man to swing through traffic.[[104]](#cite_note-Webb_rant-121)

Initially, a large stunt double was going to portay the 9-foot-tall Lizard, but Ifans insisted on playing the Lizard himself. He wore a [motion capture suit](/wiki/Motion_capture_suit "Motion capture suit"), a cardboard head and a green sleeve over his right arm. The sleeve allowed his arm to be erased in post-production, which made Connors appear to be an amputee. The motion capture process allowed even small movements—like those of Ifans' eyes and eyebrows—to be recorded and integrated into the [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery")-generated Lizard.[[s]](#cite_note-132)

*The Amazing Spider-Man* was the first Hollywood production to be filmed with the [RED Epic camera](/wiki/Red_Digital_Cinema#Cameras "Red Digital Cinema"), and was shot in 3D at 5K resolution. Cinematographer [John Schwartzman](/wiki/John_Schwartzman "John Schwartzman") said the RED cameras were crucial in achieving the type of 3D filmmaking that Webb envisioned, particularly when it came to moving the lightweight cameras on rigs for action sequences.[[97]](#cite_note-RED2-113) One of the film's climactic scenes was filmed at a 1.9:1 aspect ratio to optimize it for [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") viewing.[[114]](#cite_note-133)

The "decay rate algorithm" featured in the film was inspired by the real-life [Gompertz–Makeham law of mortality](/wiki/Gompertz%E2%80%93Makeham_law_of_mortality "Gompertz–Makeham law of mortality").[[115]](#cite_note-134) The fight scene in the subway car was influenced by the physical comedy in the films of [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") and [Buster Keaton](/wiki/Buster_Keaton "Buster Keaton").[[110]](#cite_note-AudioCom-128)

### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

Initially, *The Amazing Spider-Man* was going to be filmed in 2D, then [converted to 3D](/wiki/2D_to_3D_conversion "2D to 3D conversion") during [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production"). However, with the help of the 3D technology company [3ality Technica](/wiki/3ality_Technica "3ality Technica"), the decision was made at the last minute to shoot the film in 3D instead.[[t]](#cite_note-138) [Sony Pictures Imageworks](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Imageworks "Sony Pictures Imageworks") was responsible for a digital touch-up of the film.[[119]](#cite_note-139)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [The Amazing Spider-Man (soundtrack)](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(soundtrack) "The Amazing Spider-Man (soundtrack)")

Webb wanted a musical score that blended grandeur with intimacy, and [James Horner](/wiki/James_Horner "James Horner") was enlisted for the task.[[u]](#cite_note-143) In a review of the score for *[AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic")*, James Christopher Monger said Horner's soundtrack lacks "the pulsating gravitas" of Danny Elfman's score for the 2002 film *Spider-Man*, but evokes similar feelings. He described the score as "[m]easured, quietly grand, and at times a little old-fashioned", and felt that it focuses more on the human relationships in the film than the action sequences.[[121]](#cite_note-:1-141) Christian Clemmensen of [Filmtracks](/wiki/Filmtracks.com "Filmtracks.com") called the score "heroic when necessary but unexpectedly dramatic in its character-centric passages." He felt that the "heart" of the soundtrack is found in its piano sequences, which follow Peter's relationships "in their tender and mysterious turns".[[122]](#cite_note-:2-142) The film's [soundtrack album](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(soundtrack) "The Amazing Spider-Man (soundtrack)") was released under the [Sony Classical](/wiki/Sony_Classical "Sony Classical") label.[[123]](#cite_note-144)

## Themes and inspirations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Themes and inspirations")]

Rhys Ifans has compared Spider-Man's story to [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")* on the grounds that it can be retold in many different ways. He observed that both works depict a young man grappling with the loss of his father, and that Spider-Man can be seen as a spokesman for every generation.[[82]](#cite_note-:3-96)

Both Marc Webb and producer Avi Arad have pointed out a thematic connection between Peter Parker and Curt Connors. They noted that Peter has no parents, while Connors has no right arm. Webb said the theme of *The Amazing Spider-Man* is "we all have a missing piece", and he said Peter uses his Spider-Man persona to fill the "void" created by the loss of his parents. [[124]](#cite_note-145)

When developing *The Amazing Spider-Man*, Webb liked the idea of Oscorp being connected to everything in the story, including Peter's parents. He described Oscorp Tower as a sinister place with a mythological weight similar to the [Tower of Babel](/wiki/Tower_of_Babel "Tower of Babel").[[125]](#cite_note-Babel-146)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Marketing")]

### Merchandising and partnerships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Merchandising and partnerships")]

As part of the film's marketing campaign, various types of merchandise related to *The Amazing Spider-Man* was produced, including action figures, [PEZ](/wiki/Pez "Pez") dispensers, [Mega Bloks](/wiki/Mega_Bloks "Mega Bloks") construction sets, trading cards and nail polish.[[v]](#cite_note-157) Partnerships were developed with corporate sponsors such as [Twizzlers](/wiki/Twizzlers "Twizzlers"), the [Kellogg Company](/wiki/Kellogg_Company "Kellogg Company") and the [Keebler Company](/wiki/Keebler_Company "Keebler Company"). The fast-food restaurants [Carl's Jr.](/wiki/Carl%27s_Jr. "Carl's Jr.") and [Hardee's](/wiki/Hardee%27s "Hardee's") offered a new burger, "The Amazing Grilled Cheese Bacon Burger", which was advertised through television and radio commercials featuring *Spider-Man* co-creator [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee").[[w]](#cite_note-163) Sony launched an [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") mobile application for the film, while [D-Box Technologies](/wiki/D-Box_Technologies "D-Box Technologies") provided [motion simulation](/wiki/Motion_simulator "Motion simulator") for the film in select theaters.[[141]](#cite_note-164)[[142]](#cite_note-165) Within the film itself, [product placement](/wiki/Product_placement "Product placement") was used to advertise Sony mobile phones, tablets, monitors and laptops, as well as [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")'s [Bing](/wiki/Bing_(search_engine) "Bing (search engine)") search engine.[[143]](#cite_note-166)

### Viral campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Viral campaign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/MarkoftheSpider-Man.jpg/250px-MarkoftheSpider-Man.jpg)](/wiki/File:MarkoftheSpider-Man.jpg)

Graffiti of the *Spider-Man* logo (pictured here in [Seattle, Washington](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington")) was part of the film's viral marketing campaign in several major US cities.

A [viral marketing](/wiki/Viral_marketing "Viral marketing") campaign for the film was launched in early 2012. On January 8, the *Spider-Man* logo appeared on some of the world's most iconic buildings, such as the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House"), the [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum") of Rome, the [Kremlin](/wiki/Kremlin "Kremlin") in Moscow and the [Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Arc_de_Triomphe "Arc de Triomphe") in Paris.[[144]](#cite_note-Wonder-167)[[145]](#cite_note-Screenings-168) A "sneak-peek screening" of *The Amazing Spider-Man* was held on February 6 in 13 cities around the world.[[146]](#cite_note-169) The same month, the film's official [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") account posted [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") and [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") in major US cities, which led to comic book shops in Los Angeles, New York City, Atlanta, Denver, Seattle and Phoenix. Individuals who went on this scavenger hunt found a backpack at the location, ostensibly the property of Peter Parker.[[x]](#cite_note-173) Items in the backpacks revealed an online "countdown clock"—when the countdown was up, scavenger hunt participants were recruited to paint the *Spider-Man* logo as [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti").[[y]](#cite_note-177) The viral website also offered clues to other websites, such as a photo blog that expressed Peter Parker's point of view, and a website with instructions on how to build a web-shooter.[[z]](#cite_note-182)

The marketing campaign also included websites for [Oscorp](/wiki/Oscorp "Oscorp") and the *[Daily Bugle](/wiki/Daily_Bugle "Daily Bugle")*. The *Bugle* site featured George Stacy asking people to send in photos documenting the whereabouts of Spider-Man.[[aa]](#cite_note-186) People who uploaded images of Spider-Man received a *Spider-Man* poster.[[ab]](#cite_note-190) Through Twitter, locations were revealed that had packages containing Connors' shredded lab coat, an Oscorp identification badge and scientific samples of reptilian skin. Solving a puzzle led players to a new Oscorp website with an internship application.[[163]](#cite_note-191) Completing the application and supplying the keyword "Mutagen" revealed a Lizard-themed [featurette](/wiki/Featurette "Featurette").[[ac]](#cite_note-195) On June 1, 2012, a Carl's Jr. in [Glendale, California](/wiki/Glendale,_California "Glendale, California"), was decorated with webs and declared a "crime scene" due to Spider-Man's apprehension of a thief. Free promotional T-shirts, film passes and other prizes were distributed, and attendees were given another puzzle to solve.[[167]](#cite_note-196)[[168]](#cite_note-197) Silas Lesnick of [SuperHeroHype](/wiki/SuperheroHype! "SuperheroHype!") described the film's viral campaign as "one of the most comprehensive film virals to date".[[161]](#cite_note-lizardhunt-188)

### Philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

Leading up to the release of *The Amazing Spider-Man,* Andrew Garfield became an ambassador for the organization [Stand Up to Cancer](/wiki/Stand_Up_to_Cancer "Stand Up to Cancer"), which sold T-shirts featuring Spider-Man as part of its fundraising. Garfield explained that "these shirts underscore the idea that anyone has the power to be a hero." He described cancer as "one of our greatest villains" and said was proud to join others "in standing up to this disease."[[169]](#cite_note-198) [Laura Ziskin](/wiki/Laura_Ziskin "Laura Ziskin"), one of the film's producers, was a founder of Stand Up to Cancer and had died from breast cancer the previous year.[[170]](#cite_note-199)[[171]](#cite_note-200)

Another promotional campaign for the film was "Spider-Man Week" in New York City. Using the slogan "Be Amazing, Stand Up and Volunteer!", the campaign offered opportunities for people to be involved with various community projects, such as neighborhood beautification and CPR trainings. There were also activities featuring spiders and Spider-Man at the [New York Botanical Garden](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Garden "New York Botanical Garden"), the Wildlife Conservation Society Zoos, [Madame Tussauds New York](/wiki/Madame_Tussauds_New_York "Madame Tussauds New York"), the [Brooklyn Children's Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Children%27s_Museum "Brooklyn Children's Museum") and the [Staten Island Children's Museum](/wiki/Staten_Island_Children%27s_Museum "Staten Island Children's Museum"). To celebrate Spider-Man Week, the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") was lit in Spider-Man's characteristic colors of red and blue.[[172]](#cite_note-201)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Release")]

*The Amazing Spider-Man* had been scheduled for a July 3, 2012 worldwide release, but release dates outside North America were moved earlier to increase first-week sales.[[ad]](#cite_note-206) The worldwide premiere of the film took place in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") on Wednesday, June 13.[[177]](#cite_note-premiere-207) In North America, the film earned $7.5 million during its midnight run at 3,150 locations, including $1.2 million from 300 IMAX venues.[[178]](#cite_note-208) On its opening day, a Tuesday before the Fourth of July holiday, it set a Tuesday-gross record of $35 million, which would be surpassed in 2019 by *[Spider-Man: Far From Home](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Far_From_Home "Spider-Man: Far From Home")* with $39.3 million.[[ae]](#cite_note-212) The next day, the film dropped 33.4 percent to $23.3 million—the second highest non-opening Wednesday at the time.[[182]](#cite_note-213) Over the three-day weekend, it grossed $62 million, pushing its six-day gross to $137 million, which was smaller than the $180.07 million earned by *[Spider-Man 2](/wiki/Spider-Man_2 "Spider-Man 2")* during its Fourth of July release in 2012.[[183]](#cite_note-214)[[184]](#cite_note-215) Nevertheless, Sony stated that in "the world of re-launched franchises, this is a spectacular success by any measure".[[185]](#cite_note-216)[[186]](#cite_note-217) The film remained number one at the box office for ten consecutive days, until the opening day of *[Ice Age: Continental Drift](/wiki/Ice_Age:_Continental_Drift "Ice Age: Continental Drift")*.[[187]](#cite_note-218)

Outside North America, *The Amazing Spider-Man* grossed $51.1 million on its five-day opening weekend (June 27–July 1) from 13 markets, with strong openings in many Asian countries.[[188]](#cite_note-219) In India, it earned $6.0 million, a record opening for a Hollywood film. Its total box office gross in India was $20 million, making it the eighth highest-grossing Hollywood film in India at the time.[[af]](#cite_note-223) Opening in an additional 61 markets, the film made $127.5 million during its second weekend, and was number one at the box office in over 30 countries. In Indonesia, it broke the opening-weekend record with $4.5 million; in the United Kingdom, it opened to £11.1 million ($18.1 million).[[192]](#cite_note-224)[[193]](#cite_note-225) In China, *The Amazing Spider-Man* grossed $33.3 million during its first week, more than the lifetime gross of *[Spider-Man 3](/wiki/Spider-Man_3 "Spider-Man 3")* in that country.[[194]](#cite_note-226) China was the film's highest-grossing territory outside of North America, with $48.8 million in total.[[195]](#cite_note-227)

During its theatrical run, the film earned $262 million in North America and $495.9 million in other countries, for a worldwide total of $757.9 million.[[6]](#cite_note-BoxOffice-6) It was the [seventh highest grossing film of 2012](/wiki/2012_in_film#Highest_grossing_films "2012 in film").[[ag]](#cite_note-233) In March 2024, Sony announced that all of its live-action *Spider-Man* films would be re-released in theaters as part of Columbia Pictures' 100th anniversary celebration. *The Amazing Spider-Man* was re-released on May 6, 2024.[[201]](#cite_note-Columbia100th-234)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Home media")]

*The Amazing Spider-Man* was released by [Sony Pictures Home Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Home_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Home Entertainment") on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), [Blu-ray 3D](/wiki/Blu-ray_3D "Blu-ray 3D"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and [digital download](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") on November 9, 2012, in North America.[[202]](#cite_note-Bluray1-235) These releases contain a 90-minute behind-the-scenes documentary, audio commentary from Marc Webb, Avi Arad and Matt Tolmach, deleted scenes and other special features.[[ah]](#cite_note-240) Sony also released a limited edition gift set containing four discs along with figurines of Spider-Man and the Lizard.[[204]](#cite_note-blu-ray-237) In October 2017, the film was included in "The Spider-Man Legacy Collection", which contains five *Spider-Man* films on 4K UHD Blu-Ray.[[207]](#cite_note-241) In April 2021, Sony and Disney signed a deal to allow Sony's preceding *Spider-Man* films to stream on [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") and [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") for viewers in the United States.[[208]](#cite_note-242)[[209]](#cite_note-243) *The Amazing Spider-Man* debuted on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") on April 21, 2023.[[210]](#cite_note-Disney+Release-244)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 71% of 339 critics' reviews are positive, with an average rating of 6.6/10. The website's consensus reads: "A well-chosen cast and sure-handed direction allows *The Amazing Spider-Man* to thrill despite revisiting many of the same plot points from 2002's *[Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man_(2002_film) "Spider-Man (2002 film)")*."[[211]](#cite_note-245) Metacritic, which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigns the film a score of 66 out of 100, based on 42 critics.[[212]](#cite_note-246) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[[213]](#cite_note-247)

Boyd Van Hoeija of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* described *The Amazing Spider-Man* as a "mostly slick, entertaining and emotionally involving recombination of fresh and familiar elements".[[214]](#cite_note-Variety-248) Jordan Mintzer of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* felt the film was satisfying and had a stronger romance than *Spider-Man* (2002), and he praised the emotional and comedic touches that Webb provided.[[1]](#cite_note-thr-review-1) Writing in *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")*, Chris Pakham said the film was faithful to the comics and that Garfield's "spindly physicality evokes the Marvel illustrations of the 1960s."[[215]](#cite_note-249) Conversely, Lou Lemenick of the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* wrote that the film was "a pointless rehash in the mode of *[Superman Returns](/wiki/Superman_Returns "Superman Returns")*."[[216]](#cite_note-250) *[New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* critic [Anthony Lane](/wiki/Anthony_Lane "Anthony Lane") described *The Amazing Spider-Man* as not "amazing" and "running out of nimbleness and fun".[[217]](#cite_note-251) [Kenneth Turan](/wiki/Kenneth_Turan "Kenneth Turan") of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* called the film "memorable in pieces but not as a whole" and said the Lizard "is not quite an opponent for the ages." He felt the film's best element is the relationship between Peter and Gwen.[[218]](#cite_note-252)

[Lisa Schwarzbaum](/wiki/Lisa_Schwarzbaum "Lisa Schwarzbaum") of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* described the film as "friskier" and "sweeter-natured" than Raimi's Spider-Man films. She felt the story's intimate moments were more laudable than its spectacles.[[219]](#cite_note-253) Claudia Puig of *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* wrote that as a reboot, the film "stands on its own quite nicely, focusing more on human emotions than on a panoply of special effects." She said that Garfield's version of Spider-Man is "whip-smart and likably cheeky, with an undercurrent of teenage angst."[[220]](#cite_note-Puig-254) [Christy Lemire](/wiki/Christy_Lemire "Christy Lemire") of the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") described Garfield's Spider-Man as an arrogant and misunderstood outsider, which gives the film a "restless, reckless energy and a welcome sense of danger."[[221]](#cite_note-255) However, [Ty Burr](/wiki/Ty_Burr "Ty Burr") of *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* felt the film was "dumbed down, tarted up" and "almost shockingly uninspired" compared to the Raimi films; he described it as the worst superhero film since *[Green Lantern](/wiki/Green_Lantern_(film) "Green Lantern (film)")*.[[222]](#cite_note-256) Colin Covert of the *[Star Tribune](/wiki/Star_Tribune "Star Tribune")* also felt that *The Amazing Spider-Man* was weaker than its predecessors and described it as "*[The Notebook](/wiki/The_Notebook_(2004_film) "The Notebook (2004 film)")* in spandex".[[223]](#cite_note-257) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* felt that the reboot provided better reasons for why Peter adopts his superhero role. However, he said the Lizard was uninspired and had the dramatic range of [Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla "Godzilla").[[224]](#cite_note-Ebert-258)

[Joe Morgenstern](/wiki/Joe_Morgenstern "Joe Morgenstern") of *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* and [Dana Stevens](/wiki/Dana_Stevens_(critic) "Dana Stevens (critic)") of *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")* magazine criticized the film's retelling of Spider-Man's origin story.[[225]](#cite_note-259)[[226]](#cite_note-slate-260) However, Randy Myers from the *[San Jose Mercury News](/wiki/San_Jose_Mercury_News "San Jose Mercury News")* found it "strong, bold and well-acted", and felt it was the best *Spider-Man* film to date.[[227]](#cite_note-261) [Peter Travers](/wiki/Peter_Travers "Peter Travers") of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* wrote that the "unnecessary" reboot "pulled stellar performances from Garfield and Stone and touches the heart."[[228]](#cite_note-262) Stephanie Zacharek of [Movieline](/wiki/Movieline "Movieline") also praised the performances of Garfield and Stone.[[229]](#cite_note-Movieline-263) Tom Charity of [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") found Garfield's "combination of fresh-faced innocence, nervous agitation and wry humor ... immediately appealing."[[230]](#cite_note-CNN-264) Mary F. Pols of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* said that even though the story was familiar, Garfield and Webb made it feel "convincingly fresh and exciting."[[231]](#cite_note-265)

## Post-release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Post-release")]

### Additional films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Additional films")]

Further information: [Spider-Man in film](/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film "Spider-Man in film") and [List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films")

*The Amazing Spider-Man* was originally reported as being the first of at least three films in a series.[[240]](#cite_note-274) Leading up to the 2014 release of the first sequel, *[The Amazing Spider-Man 2](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_2 "The Amazing Spider-Man 2")*, Sony announced that two additional sequels would follow.[[241]](#cite_note-275)[[242]](#cite_note-276) However, plans for further films in the series were eventually canceled.[[243]](#cite_note-277)

In February 2015, Sony Pictures and [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") announced that Spider-Man would appear in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU).[[244]](#cite_note-SpideyMCU-278)[[245]](#cite_note-279) The MCU film *[Spider-Man: No Way Home](/wiki/Spider-Man:_No_Way_Home "Spider-Man: No Way Home")* (2021) follows Peter ([Tom Holland](/wiki/Tom_Holland "Tom Holland")) as he encounters alternate versions of himself and villains from parallel universes. [Andrew Garfield](/wiki/Andrew_Garfield "Andrew Garfield") and [Rhys Ifans](/wiki/Rhys_Ifans "Rhys Ifans") appear in the film in their respective roles of Peter and Connors from *The Amazing Spider-Man* series.[[246]](#cite_note-VillainsTrailer-280)

### Video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Video games")]

Main article: [The Amazing Spider-Man (2012 video game)](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(2012_video_game) "The Amazing Spider-Man (2012 video game)")

A video game titled [*The Amazing Spider-Man*](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(2012_video_game) "The Amazing Spider-Man (2012 video game)") was developed by [Beenox](/wiki/Beenox "Beenox"), which had developed the previous two *Spider-Man* games, *[Spider-Man: Shattered Dimensions](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Shattered_Dimensions "Spider-Man: Shattered Dimensions")* and *[Spider-Man: Edge of Time](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Edge_of_Time "Spider-Man: Edge of Time")*. *The Amazing Spider-Man* was released on June 26, 2012 for [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"), [Nintendo Wii](/wiki/Nintendo_Wii "Nintendo Wii"), [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS"), [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS") and personal computer.[[ai]](#cite_note-286) The plot of the game takes place after the events of the film.[[247]](#cite_note-shack-281) In addition, Sony and [Gameloft](/wiki/Gameloft "Gameloft") created a mobile game related to the film,[[252]](#cite_note-287) and [Sky Betting and Gaming](/wiki/Sky_Betting_and_Gaming "Sky Betting and Gaming")'s [online casino](/wiki/Online_casino "Online casino") website Sky Vegas released a related [casino game](/wiki/Casino_game "Casino game").[[253]](#cite_note-288)

### Comic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man_(film)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Comic")]

In June 2012, a two-part comic titled *The Amazing Spider-Man: The Movie* #1–2 was released. It is written by Tom Cohen and illustrated by Neil Edwards.[[254]](#cite_note-289) A week before the film's release, a trade paperback titled *The Amazing Spider-Man: The Movie Prelude* was published. It contains the two-part comic, as well as *[The Amazing Spider-Man](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man "The Amazing Spider-Man")* issues #75–77.[[255]](#cite_note-290)
